metadata.from: James Edward Hanifen Sr. (Dad) — signed 'Dad' at the end, writing to his son Jimmie/Jim Jr.
metadata.to: Jimmie (James Edward Hanifen Jr., Bob's brother)
metadata.location_written_from: Likely Des Moines, Iowa (D.M. referenced in letter)
topics: Bob's military/ASTP address at University of Wyoming
sending money and supplies to Bob
soldiers going home sick from Dakota University
metadata.mood_tone: Conversational, somewhat frustrated and world-weary, paternal concern mixed with dry humor; Dad is venting about the complexity of Army life and giving practical advice.
metadata.legibility_notes: Cursive handwriting is somewhat difficult to parse in places; some words required guessing. Abbreviations and phonetic spellings used throughout (e.g., 'no' for 'know', 'dam' for 'damn', 'nuf' for 'enough', 'rite' for 'right'). The address block '17470600' and 'ASTP' are partially legible.
metadata.historical_context: Letter references ASTP (Army Specialized Training Program), a WWII-era program that sent soldiers to universities for accelerated academic training. Bob appears to be stationed at the University of Wyoming in Laramie as part of this program. The letter also references soldiers being sent back home from Dakota University due to homesickness and then being told to wait for the draft, reflecting wartime mobilization anxieties.

pages.transcription:
July 13 - 44

Dear Jimmie

     Well I dont know how long this letter laid out here, so Ill try + give you Bob's add, Just thought, I cant give it all to you B.A. —: 17470600.
A.S.T.R.P. + there is a Unit No Laramie Wyo, also University of Wyo. + that is not all, He has to get Stamps on his letters, Isnt that Something. From the way it looks, you dont no more get out of one thing, until you get into another. That is a sure way to go Nuts, or get into dam good Trouble. You just as well no it. One Camp is just as bad as the other. Bob starts off at 6 AM + goes right thru untill 10:30 PM. In about a 3 weeks, He will no he is in Hell too. Ill bet a Buck - You when you get this, at lease rite. + Say I got it - 2 words arent a hell

of a lot to say. To me yet means, $500 saved. as it is Gamble to send it this way Bay. Bob dont get a dam penny while in school, even how to Send him Towels, + I suppose I will have to send him Soap + Toilet paper. 2 guys that went to Dakota University, Got Home Sick, so they let them Come Back to D.M. the next day, a letter came + Told them to Stay there, and Wait for the draft. Well the Kids Wouldnt have left, if they had Knowen that, But they Say it is realy Tough in those Schools - Well this is a nuf of this B. at this time, all I got to do now, is find Somthing to put this in 50 - 50 Long for this time

Dad -

full_transcription:
July 13 - 44

Dear Jimmie

     Well I dont know how long this letter laid out here, so Ill try + give you Bob's add, Just thought, I cant give it all to you B.A. —: 17470600.

From the way it looks, you dont no more get out of one thing, until you get into another. That is a sure way to go Nuts, or get into dam good Trouble. You just as well no it. One Camp is just as bad as the other. Bob starts off at 6 AM + goes right thru untill 10:30 PM. In about a 3 weeks, He will no he is in Hell too. Ill bet a Buck - You when you get this, at lease rite. + Say I got it - 2 words arent a hell of a lot to say. To me yet means, $500 saved. as it is Gamble to send it this way Bay. Bob dont get a dam penny while in school, even how to Send him Towels, + I suppose I will have to send him Soap + Toilet paper. 2 guys that went to Dakota University, Got Home Sick, so they let them Come Back to D.M. the next day, a letter came + Told them to Stay there, and Wait for the draft. summary: In this July 1944 letter, James Hanifen Sr. (Dad) writes to his son Jimmie (Jim Jr.) to pass along Bob's military address at the ASTP program at the University of Wyoming in Laramie. Dad vents about the grueling nature of Bob's training schedule (6 AM to 10:30 PM) and the financial strain of supporting Bob, who receives no pay while in school. He also recounts a cautionary tale about two soldiers who left Dakota University due to homesickness only to be told to wait for the draft, warning that leaving these programs has serious consequences. The letter is signed simply 'Dad' and reflects a tone of paternal concern, dry humor, and working-class pragmatism.
